sentences.text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the first part of the question is very easy to answer.
She came a year after I did, in '48.
When we decided to get married is a-- is a subject of great contention between the two of us because I think it was in the early '30s, and she thinks it was in the mid-'30s.
But we knew each other in Budapest.
And-- and we knew we would get married.
Well, I was born in '28.
So in the mid '30s, I was seven, which, you know, you grow up fast in that [LAUGHS] old country world.
And-- and-- we grew up together, but, of course, I grew old and she grew young.
So now there's a huge time difference between us, a gigantic age gap.
But it's-- we have been married now almost 47 years.
And the first 47 years is quite good.
So we are looking forward to the next 47.
We have two daughters and we have 17 grandchildren. 17.
And the reason is that our two daughters decided that since our families were basically killed in the Holocaust, they wanted to create a new family for us.
And they have.
And it's clearly our [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] greatest joy and the focus of our-- of our lives.
Annette was instrumental in my becoming involved with human rights.
She really is the person who did 99% of the work, for instance, on Raoul Wallenberg.
The Raoul Wallenberg project began on the kitchen table in our kitchen.
And Annette gave years of her life to it.
She then suggested when I was elected to Congress that I introduce legislation making him an honorary citizen.
I got the credit, but she did the work.
Later on, she decided that the Holocaust Museum in Washington be placed on Raoul Wallenberg Place, so we went through the process of renaming that street.
She did it.
Then, not long ago she succeeded in having a bust of Raoul Wallenberg placed in the capital, which was a tremendous achievement.
Hundreds of people whose lives Raoul saved flew in from Australia, New Zealand, Europe, Canada, over the United States, Hungary.
Then she worked on a Raoul Wallenberg stamp.
Soon in every post office, you'll be able to buy Raoul Wallenberg stamp, which is her doing.
She has given much of her life to it.
And she has been my inspiration in the work of human rights.
Yes.
Very much so.
Very much so.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You don't need to be a rocket scientist to realize how much power there is in the political arena.
A Hitler, a Stalin, a Saddam Hussein, a Gaddafi-- or conversely for good.
Great political leaders can-- can achieve wonderful things and bad political leaders can create a nightmare.
And since money was never very important to me, I felt I wanted to be in public service.
So for 33 years I was in university teaching, which is a form of public service.
And for the last 16, I've been in Congress, which is another form of public service.
And I-- I wouldn't trade a minute of these 49 years for anything.
Well, it's not my achievements.
In the first place, Annette and I have worked as a team on every project.
Secondly, when you are in government in a democratic society, you realize that you don't achieve anything.
You can only get anyplace by motivating your colleagues and lots of other people to-- to work with you.

But I would say when my congressional career is over, which I hope is a long ways from now, it will be my work on the field of human rights and a secure Israel, peace in the Middle East, respect for all human beings, irrespective of race and religion and sex and ethnicity.
It's not difficult for me to pick the field where I may have made a very modest contribution, and that field is basically human rights.
I'm extremely well accepted, and I'm extremely comfortable.

As a matter of fact, my background and my experiences-- and all of my colleagues not only know them but know a great deal about them, as well as the president and the vice president, and the previous president, and the previous president-- my experiences give me a voice of authority on these subjects.
American government works in very strange ways.
We pride ourselves of being a democratic government.
But at the same time, we are looking for experts.
So if you have in Congress a-- an eminent scientist, his voice is listened to on matters of science.
My voice is listened to on matters that relate to-- to my concerns.
When I talk about Louis Farrakhan, who spreads venom and antisemitism, and I introduce a resolution condemning his speeches, my colleagues listen to me because of who I am.
When I deal with a subject like Iran or Iraq or Libya, my colleagues listen to me because they understand my long term opposition to dictatorial regimes.
So I would say that, while I certainly didn't ask for this background, my background is an asset, not a liability.
I want to see the Middle East peace process continue.
I'm spending a lot of my time and energy on it.
I believe that the region can be made more stable, less turbulent, less violent.

And in whatever way I can, and I spend a lot of time in the Arab countries-- in Syria, in Jordan, in Egypt, in Oman-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I-- I believe the United States has a tremendously important responsibility in this area.
And I try as best I can to-- to bring a voice of reason and compassion, and an understanding of all the many complex elements that play in the Middle East.
But I am deeply committed to the peace process.
I don't think there is any other option in the Middle East.
Well, look, it's always the president of the United States who plays the greatest role.

The United States is the one remaining superpower, and our president, whether it's Bill Clinton or George Bush or Ronald Reagan or Jimmy Carter or going back, these-- these-- he is-- or she is-- the most powerful force in the world.
And I believe this will be the case for a long, long time to come.
And I'm very grateful for that because I view American influence to be an enormously benign influence in the world.
It's very fashionable to be critical of the United States.
And we obviously have made mistakes.
But on a comparative scale, we are by far the greatest force for peace and for decency on this much troubled planet.
I'm doing my best to strengthen the influence of the United States, both in the Middle East and globally.
That's why I serve on the International Relations Committee.
That's-- that's my life.
I'm very committed to my family.
I-- I spent an unbelievable amount of time with our two daughters.
Still try to.
And I'm now spending every spare minute I have with-- with our grandchildren.
Annette and I usually have one or two or three or four with us.
If we don't have any with us, we go visit one family or the other.
And our whole life revolves around them.
It's-- we are enormously fortunate.
We-- we lost some grandchildren, but we have 17 living grandchildren and we-- we feel rich beyond words.
One family lives in Denver, and one family lives in New Hampshire.
And I-- I think I am honest in saying-- I think I'm accurate in saying I have a very personal relationship with every single one of those children.
I mean, I'm deeply involved in their lives, in-- in-- in every aspect of their lives.
They seem to tolerate me.
Oh yes, very much so.
Not only did they know about me as a Holocaust survivor, we visited countless Holocaust sites in Europe.
My work took me to Europe several months every year for 10 years.
They came with us to Israel on countless occasions.
And from Yad Vashem to Kibbutzim, they have been deeply immersed in our background and in our lives.
And one of our daughters attended the Hebrew University in Jerusalem.
And both of our daughters are passionately committed to the same political and human rights concerns that Annette and I share.
Very much so.
The same way.
By now, I think-- I want to be accurate-- I think almost a half of them have been to visit Holocaust sites.
Several of them have been to Israel.
As a matter of fact, just this last September, our oldest granddaughter spent some time with us in Israel again.
And-- and less than a year ago, our younger daughter and her husband and their baby came to Israel with us.

So we-- we-- we make our lives an integral part of their lives, and our lives were defined early on by the Holocaust, and the last 50 years in the value of a free and open society and the prevention of any future nightmares.
Well, Annette is a Holocaust survivor in a different way.
She received protection from the Portuguese minister to Hungary, who received his inspiration from Wallenberg.
And she left with the Portuguese minister, Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], during the most difficult period of 1944 for Switzerland.
So in a sense, Raoul Wallenberg saved my life directly and Raoul Wallenberg saved Annette's life indirectly.

I need to tell you that when the Holocaust Museum opened and there was a special children's program, one of our granddaughters, Chelsea, played what to me was an unbelievably moving role in that children's program.
She wrote a letter to Raoul Wallenberg, thanking him for saving the lives of her grandfather and grandmother so her mother could be born and so that she could be born.

And when we placed Raoul Wallenberg-- Wallenberg's statue in the Capital, there were five speakers-- the speaker of our Congress, the speaker of the Israeli Knesset, the speaker of the Hungarian Parliament, the speaker of the Swedish Parliament, and our granddaughter, Chelsea.
So it was very difficult for us not to-- not to cry very hard because it was an incredibly moving moment.
It was on national television, and we all felt that this 12-year-old girl captured the essence of what Raoul Wallenberg was probably better than the many books that have been written about him.
It's very simple, [?

Renee. ?] We are our brothers' and sister' keepers, whether these are Buddhists in Burma or Tibet, whether these are Christians who are persecuted, whether these are people who are persecuted because of their pigmentation-- the color of their skin-- whether they are persecuted because of their ethnic background like the nightmares we had been through in the former Yugoslavia, we need to speak up and stop these outrages because the bell always sounds for us.
We cannot be bystanders.
It's not enough not to be a perpetrator.
You must not be a bystander.
The prime obligation of every human being is to speak out against injustice committed against any other human being.
And the more different that human being is from you, the greater your obligation to speak out for that human being and to defend him.
Raoul Wallenberg was not Hungarian.
He was not Jewish.
He was not persecuted.

He was a-- he was a Christian Swede who was very wealthy and very safe and very secure, and yet he left all of that behind and risked his life, and paid with his life for this message-- that we are our brother's keeper.
No, I don't think there is anything you didn't ask me, but I want to say that I can't begin to thank Steven Spielberg and the Shoah Project for doing this.
What you're doing personally, what Steven is doing, what-- what all of your associates are doing is one of the great historic gestures to make this world a more sane place.
And as a-- as a humble worker in the vineyard of this enterprise, I want to thank you very much for doing it.
It's a pleasure.
